C[saxmaam: Something I've been wondering.   What kind of bug was causing that line 19 stone placement problem on ios?

C[anoek: In one of our event handlers we were comparing the equality of event target to make sure we were still on the canvas or something of the sort.. except what we were comparing was sometimes null, and we were testing against something else that was sometimes undefined
anoek: and we were using == instead of ===
anoek: So yeah.. a couple years of pain from a missing = sign :(
C[saxmaam: Oh.....! Like a pointer error in C.  Thanks.  I've been wondering what that was about forever

kevincoleman: If he sees it, I should treat this group as alive and just guard against it expanding. If he doesn’t, I should attack this group as though killing it until he abandons it or I can tenuki to kill.
kevincoleman: But the real question: what makes me a stronger player?
kevincoleman: I remember a proverb about never assuming you e see something you’r your opponent doesn’t. That makes sense here.

kevincoleman: So, if I were a stronger player I’d be asking not whether I should try to kill this group, but how I can benefit from it the most. Even keeping it barely alive could just get me more free moves than if it were dead.
kevincoleman: So, if I can play moves that are primarily beneficial to me while still keeping adequate pressure on the group, bingo.
kevincoleman: Then if I can get the tenuki to kill it’s a bonus.

C[blindgorgon: Unconventional, but let’s try it.

C[blindgorgon: Note to find the better attack here after the game. G19 secures the life of the stones, but otherwise feels slow. L16 and M16 don’t feel like quite enough to kill the top group via a race if he tries to save it though. Does Q17 help win that race? 🤔
blindgorgon: The conundrum is this: play as though he doesn’t see the way to save the top stones or play as though he does?
blindgorgon: K15 definitely didn’t save them, but the question is if K15 was better than saving them (in which case it shows that he saw the saving move and played bigger)
blindgorgon: If K15 is not a better play than saving the stones it might indicate that he didn’t see the save.
blindgorgon: BUT THEN. Go is an objective game. Regardless of intents of the player the best move on the board is still the same.
blindgorgon: Am I trying to beat the player, or beat the game?
blindgorgon: New idea. Don’t wait til after the game to find the best move. 🤣
C[blindgorgon: This way if he tries the save I have the cut already, and it adds some thickness to my stones on line O
C[blindgorgon: Hm. This could have been better. It threatens K15 & L16 directly, but induces G14. Is that a good enough exchange?
blindgorgon: The timing was just wrong. I should have split the left side first so it makes two weak groups when attacking.
C[blindgorgon: Oh man that worked too well. Ideal place for thickness to attack with.
C[blindgorgon: It’s already rough, but if he doesn’t protect the left side this is pretty well over. :(
blindgorgon: I guess that center group would hurt to lose too. Two weak groups in a big way, so there may be no recovery here.
C[blindgorgon: F14 helps dig out of the weak group problem a bit. It either resolves the false eye issue at H15 or provides a little strength to the C16 wall.

blindgorgon: I should review this response. E14 actively threatens the wall at C16, but it pretty much guarantees life in the middle. Given the potential that remins on the left side this seems like the right choice, but it doesn’t let me milk the two weak groups for very long. Maybe better to just take the large corner and then sit back for the rest of the game.
blindgorgon: I guess he could still ignore the center to support the C16 wall at C14 or something, but that’s not a clear victory, as a ponnuki at F14 would be massive a problem to deal with.
C[blindgorgon: Would be a time saver to be able to plan conditional moves without the analysis tool available, but I get the conflict…
